structured_choices.data_source: 10,000+ high-resolution microscopy images of tissue biopsies collected from multiple hospitals over a two-year period. Images are sourced from routine clinical workflows and represent a cross-section of patient populations and specimen types expected in deployment.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label (cancerous / non-cancerous) assigned by experienced pathologists during routine clinical review. To account for labeling inconsistency, a subset of 500 images (stratified by image source hospital and initial label) will be re-reviewed by a panel of 3 independent pathologists; consensus labels (≥2 of 3 agreement) are used as ground truth for these 500 images. Images with initial pathologist label only are retained for training but flagged during analysis to assess their impact on model generalization.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split by (hospital site, class label). The dataset is first stratified by the 3–5 hospital sites of origin and by class (cancerous/non-cancerous) to preserve the site-specific imaging condition variability and class balance across splits. No patient-level stratification is applied because individual patient identifiers are not available; however, images are assumed to be independent specimens. Split percentages: 70% train (7,000 images), 15% validation (1,500 images), 15% test (1,500 images). Stratification justifies that each split represents the hospital-site and class distribution in the source population, reducing confounding by imaging equipment or staining protocol differences between hospitals. A stratified split also ensures that if class imbalance is severe, both train and test sets reflect it proportionally.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (image normalization, augmentation parameters if used) are fit on the training set only. Normalization statistics (e.g., per-channel mean and std) are computed from train images and applied identically to validation and test sets. No augmentation is applied to validation or test sets; augmentation (if used) is applied only during training to prevent inflating validation/test accuracy with synthetic variants.
structured_choices.features: Raw feature set: 8-bit or 16-bit pixel intensities across all channels of the high-resolution images. No handcrafted feature extraction is performed for the CNN. For the baseline (traditional feature extraction), features are engineered as follows: (1) Color histograms (32 bins per channel, 96 features total), (2) Texture features (Gabor filter responses at 4 orientations and 3 scales, 12 features), (3) Local Binary Pattern (LBP) histograms (59 bins, 59 features), (4) Haralick texture features (13 features from gray-level co-occurrence matrices). All handcrafted features are computed on the raw image or its grayscale conversion; feature scaling (standardization to zero mean, unit variance) is fit on the training set and applied to val/test. For the CNN, images are resized to a consistent resolution (e.g., 256×256 or 512×512, justified by input size constraints and computational budget) and pixel intensities are normalized to [0, 1] or standardized using training set statistics. Data augmentation (rotation ±15°, horizontal flip, slight brightness/contrast jitter) is applied only during training.
structured_choices.model: CNN architecture: ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on the training set. The last fully connected layer is replaced with a binary classification head (sigmoid output). Justification: ResNet-50 provides strong inductive bias for image classification and is well-established in medical imaging; transfer learning from ImageNet reduces the sample size burden compared to training from scratch. Alternative architecture (DenseNet-121) is considered but ResNet-50 is selected as the primary model to ensure reproducibility and computational efficiency.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic Regression trained on handcrafted features (color histograms, Gabor, LBP, Haralick features as defined above). Justification: Logistic Regression is the traditional benchmark in medical image analysis prior to deep learning adoption. It is given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter C is selected via grid search over {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100} using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set; class weights are balanced to account for imbalance (weight inversely proportional to class frequency). Both models use the same train/val/test split and are evaluated on the same metrics. The baseline receives the same preprocessing rigor (scaling fit on train only, applied to val/test) as the CNN. If class imbalance is detected (e.g., <20% cancerous), the baseline is also evaluated with balanced class weights during training to ensure fairness.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Sensitivity (recall for the cancerous class): TP / (TP + FN). Justification: The stakeholder context explicitly prioritizes minimizing false negatives (missed cancer diagnoses), as the clinical cost of a false negative (delayed diagnosis) far exceeds a false positive (additional review by pathologist). Sensitivity directly measures the model's ability to catch cancerous cases. Sensitivity is computed on the held-out test set and is the primary metric for model selection and claims.
secondary_metrics: Specificity (true negative rate): TN / (TN + FP), to ensure the model does not generate excessive false alarms that burden pathologists.
Balanced Accuracy: (Sensitivity + Specificity) / 2, as a summary metric robust to class imbalance.

Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC): to assess discrimination performance across all classification thresholds and to allow threshold adjustment if deployment requires specific sensitivity/specificity trade-offs.
F1-score (macro-averaged if highly imbalanced): harmonic mean of precision and recall, as secondary validation of balance between false positives and false negatives.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping decisions are made using the validation set (1,500 images). The CNN is trained on the training set with early stopping monitored on validation sensitivity; training stops if validation sensitivity does not improve for 10 epochs. For the baseline (Logistic Regression), hyperparameter C is selected via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set using sensitivity as the selection criterion. Once hyperparameters are fixed (on validation data for CNN; via cross-validation for baseline), the final model is re-trained on the combined training + validation sets (9,500 images) and evaluated once on the held-out test set. This maximizes training data while using the validation set only for model selection, not for final evaluation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (1,500 images, 15% of total) is held out and never touched during model development, hyperparameter tuning, or feature engineering. A single evaluation pass is performed on the test set at the end. Test set results are reported alongside 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity and specificity (computed via binomial exact test or bootstrap). The test set includes images from all hospital sites and both classes in stratified proportions, ensuring the test evaluation is representative of the deployment population.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Labeling inconsistency: 500 images are re-labeled by a 3-pathologist panel; consensus labels replace original labels for these images. The remaining 9,500 images retain single-pathologist labels but are flagged. A sensitivity analysis reports model performance separately on (a) the 500 consensus-labeled images and (b) the 9,500 single-labeled images to assess robustness to labeling noise. Imaging condition variability (staining, equipment differences across hospitals): Stratified random split ensures each train/val/test set contains a representative mix of images from each hospital site. Additional analysis reports per-hospital accuracy to detect site-specific model degradation. If site-specific performance is heterogeneous (e.g., sensitivity varies by >10% across sites), this is noted and confounding by imaging protocol is acknowledged. The experiment establishes whether CNN outperforms traditional features on this dataset; it does not establish whether the CNN generalizes to unseen hospitals or imaging protocols (external validation is a separate study).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Traditional Features for Cancer Cell Detection in Microscopy

### 1. Data Strategy and Labeling

This experiment uses 10,000+ high-resolution microscopy images collected from multiple hospital sites over two years. Each image is labeled for the presence or absence of cancerous cells by trained pathologists. To account for known labeling inconsistency, a subset of 500 images will be independently reviewed by a panel of three pathologists; consensus labels (≥2 of 3 agreement) replace the original single-pathologist labels for these images. The remaining 9,500 images retain their original labels but are tracked separately during analysis to quantify the effect of labeling noise on generalization.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

The dataset is divided using a stratified random split by (hospital site, class label). This strategy ensures that:
- Imaging condition variability across hospitals is represented proportionally in train, validation, and test sets.
- Class imbalance (if present) is preserved in each split, preventing artificial balance that would not reflect deployment conditions.
- No temporal ordering is imposed (images are not time-ordered by acquisition date within this split, as clinical deployment does not require temporal generalization).

Split proportions: 70% train (7,000 images), 15% validation (1,500 images), 15% test (1,500 images). The test set is held out and used only once, at the end, for final evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**CNN (proposed model):** Raw pixel intensities are the input. Images are resized to a consistent 256×256 or 512×512 resolution (justified by computational budget and standard practice in medical imaging). Pixel values are normalized to [0, 1] using statistics (mean, std) computed on the training set. During training, mild augmentation is applied (rotation ±15°, horizontal flip, brightness/contrast jitter) to reduce overfitting; no augmentation is applied to validation or test data.

**Baseline (traditional features):** The following handcrafted features are extracted from each image:
- Color histograms (32 bins per channel, 96 features).
- Gabor filter responses (4 orientations, 3 scales, 12 features).
- Local Binary Pattern (LBP) histograms (59 features).
- Haralick texture features (13 features from co-occurrence matrices).

Total: ~180 handcrafted features. Features are standardized (zero mean, unit variance) using statistics fit on the training set only; the same transformation is applied to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and ensures fair comparison.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed model:** ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. The final fully connected layer is replaced with a binary classification head (sigmoid output). Transfer learning is justified because it leverages general image representations and reduces sample size requirements.

**Baseline:** Logistic Regression trained on the handcrafted features above. This represents the traditional diagnostic approach prior to deep learning. Hyperparameter C is tuned via grid search on {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100} using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set, with sensitivity as the selection criterion. Class weights are balanced (inverse class frequency weighting) to account for imbalance.

Both models receive equivalent care: hyperparameter tuning, class weighting, and identical data preprocessing.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Sensitivity (recall for cancerous class) = TP / (TP + FN). This directly aligns with the stakeholder's priority: minimizing false negatives (missed cancers) is clinically critical. Sensitivity is reported on the held-out test set with 95% confidence intervals.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Specificity** = TN / (TN + FP): ensures the model does not generate excessive false alarms.
- **Balanced Accuracy** = (Sensitivity + Specificity) / 2: summary metric robust to imbalance.
- **AUC-ROC:** discrimination across all thresholds; supports threshold tuning if deployment requirements change.
- **F1-score** (macro-averaged): precision-recall trade-off.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used exclusively for model selection:
- The CNN is trained on the training set (7,000 images) with early stopping based on validation sensitivity (patience = 10 epochs).
- Logistic Regression hyperparameters are selected via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set using sensitivity.

Once hyperparameters are fixed, both models are re-trained on the combined training + validation sets (9,500 images) to maximize use of available data. Final evaluation is performed once on the held-out test set (1,500 images).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

**Labeling inconsistency:** A 3-pathologist consensus panel re-labels 500 randomly selected images. Sensitivity analysis reports performance on consensus-labeled vs. single-labeled subsets. If performance diverges significantly (e.g., >5% sensitivity drop on consensus labels), this indicates labeling noise is a confound.

**Imaging condition variability:** Stratified split ensures each set represents all hospital sites. Per-hospital accuracy is reported to detect site-specific model degradation. If site-specific sensitivity varies by >10%, confounding by imaging protocol is documented.

**Scope of claims:** This experiment establishes whether CNN outperforms traditional features on this dataset under these imaging conditions. It does not establish generalization to unseen hospitals, imaging equipment, or staining protocols; external validation is a separate study.

### 8. Expected Outcome

The experiment compares CNN sensitivity vs. Logistic Regression sensitivity on the held-out test set. A CNN advantage is supported if CNN sensitivity is statistically significantly higher (e.g., by binomial test or bootstrap resampling) and clinically meaningful (e.g., >2–3 percentage point improvement, subject to stakeholder input).
